content.text:
TCP and UDP are the two main transport-layer protocols in the internet stack. The core difference is **reliability vs. speed**.

## TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)

Connection-oriented and reliable. It does a lot of bookkeeping to guarantee delivery:

- **Handshake first** — establishes a connection (the "3-way handshake": SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) before any data flows.
- **Guaranteed delivery** — every segment is acknowledged; lost segments are retransmitted.
- **Ordered** — data arrives in the order it was sent, reassembled correctly even if packets take different paths.
- **Flow & congestion control** — adapts sending rate to avoid overwhelming the receiver or the network.
- **Higher overhead** — more header bytes, more round-trips, added latency.

**Used by:** HTTP/HTTPS (web), SSH, email (SMTP), file transfer — anything where correctness matters more than latency.

## UDP (User Datagram Protocol)

Connectionless and "fire-and-forget." It just sends packets (datagrams) and hopes they arrive:

- **No handshake** — send immediately, no connection setup.
- **No delivery guarantee** — packets can be lost, duplicated, or arrive out of order, and UDP won't fix it.
- **No ordering** — the application must handle reordering if it cares.
- **Minimal overhead** — tiny 8-byte header, no ACKs, very low latency.

**Used by:** DNS, video/voice streaming, VoIP, online games, DHCP — anything where speed and low latency beat perfect delivery (a dropped video frame is better than a stalled one).

## Quick comparison

| | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Yes (handshake) | No |
| Reliability | Guaranteed | Best-effort |
| Ordering | Preserved | Not guaranteed |
| Speed/latency | Slower, more overhead | Fast, lean |
| Header size | 20+ bytes | 8 bytes |
| Use case | Web, SSH, email | Streaming, gaming, DNS |

**Rule of thumb:** use TCP when you can't afford to lose data, UDP when you can't afford to wait. Modern protocols like QUIC (which HTTP/3 runs on) build reliability *on top of* UDP to get the best of both.

Want me to go deeper on any part — the handshake, congestion control, or how QUIC changes the picture?
